metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the design (spike story or gate-wiring story) exactly how max_retries=0 is enforced on the normal (model-string) code path where pydantic-ai constructs AnthropicProvider internally, not just on the paths where runner.py explicitly instantiates AnthropicProvider.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'SDK max_retries=0' as an AC but does not specify how this is enforced on the default pydantic-ai code path, where no explicit AnthropicProvider is constructed today and the Anthropic SDK's default max_retries (2) is silently inherited. The plan's injection discussion only covers the AnthropicProvider constructor parameters; it doesn't say where max_retries=0 is passed or how it is set on the model-string path where pydantic-ai builds its own provider internally.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — Transport-retry dependency + injection point; Acceptance Criteria: 'Retry is owned at exactly one layer (the httpx transport), SDK max_retries=0'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a named fallback design for the transport-retry layer (e.g., a bespoke `httpx.AsyncTransport` subclass using `tenacity` directly) to be activated if the spike (story 95a5) determines that `pydantic_ai.retries.AsyncTenacityTransport` is absent or unsuitable, so a negative spike result does not block all 7 child stories.
findings.finding: The `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` extra and `pydantic_ai.retries.AsyncTenacityTransport` / `wait_retry_after` are the foundational dependency for the entire retry architecture, but the plan names NO fallback design if the spike (story 95a5) fails to confirm these exist as described. Three prior reviews flagged this as nonexistent; the plan dismisses those as 'code-blind' and provides a probe command, but the probe cannot be run in this review environment (evidence class: Unverified). The plan calls this a 'hard prerequisite' yet the epic has 7 child stories with no stated contingency if the prerequisite is absent.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point'; AC bullet: 'pydantic-ai-slim[retries] is added as a dependency'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Architecture constraints section, add: 'Spike failure path: if `pydantic_ai.retries.AsyncTenacityTransport` is not available as described, the transport-retry layer falls back to a hand-rolled `AsyncTenacityTransport`-equivalent built directly on `tenacity` + `httpx.AsyncBaseTransport`, injected via the same `AnthropicProvider(http_client=)` path. The spike result selects the implementation; the rest of the epic is unaffected.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve ADR ownership for the deferred async watchdog: either grant fd1d authority to record it in its wave AND have 2eca reference/adopt it, or explicitly state fd1d's CI gate waives that AC bullet until 2eca lands (and document the cross-wave dependency).
findings.finding: child fd1d's Acceptance Criteria independently require it to record the deferred async stream-event watchdog ADR ('An ADR records the deferred async stream-event watchdog + its migration trigger'), but child 2eca declares itself the 'sole ADR owner' for that same ADR and lands in merge wave 6 (last), while fd1d is in wave {2,3,4,5}. fd1d's AC cannot be verified green in isolation before 2eca lands, yet the plan presents these as independently CI-verifiable wave members.
findings.location: child fd1d-5f0d-4bd8-40f4 — Acceptance Criteria bullet 5; child 2eca-0a1d-45c2-4e38 — 'ADRs recorded HERE (sole owner)' and 'Per-class actionable messages + exit code 11. ADRs recorded HERE (sole owner): exit-11 contract, diagnostic-sanitization contract, deferred async watchdog.'
findings.suggested_fix: Give fd1d a stub/placeholder ADR file it creates (with a 'DRAFT — final rationale in 2eca' marker), and have 2eca expand and finalize it. Alternatively, remove the ADR bullet from fd1d's AC and add an explicit 'depends-on: 2eca' dependency edge for that criterion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the transport-injection design addresses BOTH AnthropicProvider construction sites in runner.py (the normal path and the existing loopback-bypass branch at lines 233-240), so AsyncTenacityTransport is wired on both paths and neither silently loses retry coverage.
findings.finding: The transport-injection mechanism doesn't account for the existing loopback-proxy-bypass code path in runner.py:233-240, which already constructs a custom AnthropicProvider(base_url=_direct). If AsyncTenacityTransport is injected only on the 'normal' (no loopback bypass) path, users in a loopback-proxy environment silently lose transport-layer retry coverage; if injected only on the bypass path, normal users lose it. The plan's Architecture constraints section verifies the injection point signature but doesn't address this pre-existing branch.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — Transport-retry dependency + injection point; runner.py:233-240
findings.suggested_fix: In the gate-wiring story (or the spike), extract a helper `_make_anthropic_provider(base_url=None, http_client=None)` that always passes `http_client=<AsyncTenacityTransport instance>` alongside any `base_url`, so both the normal path and the `_local_proxy_bypass_base_url` branch share the same transport-injected provider construction.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the inter-story interface for result-path classification: specify that 978d constructs a synthetic LLMOutcome (or calls a designated factory in 30ff's module) rather than setting coverage.resolution_class directly, so all classification flows through the single sanitized path.
findings.finding: child 978d detects refusal-as-content via result validation and routes it to resolution_class=CHANGE_INPUT, but does not specify whether it constructs an LLMOutcome via 30ff's classify_llm_failure or independently sets coverage.resolution_class. If 978d independently constructs the classification, there are two parallel routing paths (exception path via 30ff, result-validation path via 978d) that can diverge in field naming, sanitization, or diagnostic structure — and neither child defines the handoff interface.
findings.location: child 978d-1d5d-8287-47bc — 'A detected refusal-as-content routes to resolution_class = CHANGE_INPUT (from story 30ff's vocabulary) and is surfaced via coverage, not returned as a PASS/BLOCK verdict'; child 30ff-21a3-0724-422b — classifier covers ContentFilterError / content_filter finish_reason on the exception path

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fourth required ADR (in AC and gate-wiring story) for the transport-retry architecture: why retries live at the httpx-transport layer, the SDK `max_retries=0` invariant, the side-effect-safety argument, and what would need to change if the retry layer moved.
findings.finding: The transport-retry architecture — retrying at the httpx transport layer with SDK max_retries=0, justified by side-effect-safety because already-executed tools are not repeated — is a novel architectural decision that is not listed among the required ADRs. Future maintainers who consider changing the retry layer (e.g., moving to SDK-level or agent-level retries) need to find the invariants, constraints, and reasoning in plan prose rather than a formal ADR.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions (transport-retry section); Acceptance Criteria — ADR bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the AC ADR bullet: '…and the transport-retry layer choice (httpx-transport vs SDK vs agent-graph, side-effect-safety invariant, max_retries=0 rationale).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Standardize on one term ('signed' or 'certified') for the structural guard concept throughout the plan — or add a one-line parenthetical in the first use: e.g., 'signed (i.e., certified)' — so all sections and child-story ACs reference the same token.
findings.finding: The plan uses both 'signed' and 'certify/certified' interchangeably for the same concept (the structural guard preventing a degraded verdict from being approved), creating terminology drift across sections.
findings.location: Design bullets ('Never certify a degraded result (structural guard on signing)'), Success Criteria ('never produce a signed/certified verdict'), AC[6] ('a degraded/INDETERMINATE verdict can never be signed (structural guard)')
findings.suggested_fix: Pick 'signed' (already the more precise/implementable term) and replace 'certify/certified' with 'sign/signed' in the Design bullets and Success Criteria, adding a note at first use: 'signed = the cryptographic/approval stamp that makes a verdict authoritative'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a startup-time guard (e.g., assert the injected transport type on the constructed httpx client, or verify `max_retries` is 0 only when transport presence is confirmed) so that a failed injection is loud rather than silently degrading retry behavior to worse-than-baseline.
findings.finding: Setting `max_retries=0` on the Anthropic SDK while relying on `AsyncTenacityTransport` injection means that if the transport injection silently fails at runtime (e.g., wrong initialization path, import error of the optional extra), retries drop from the current default of 2 to 0 — making failure handling *worse* than today. The plan specifies a spike (95a5) to verify the happy path but does not require a startup guard or assertion that confirms injection succeeded.
findings.location: Architecture constraints — 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point' paragraph; Acceptance Criteria bullet 2 ('SDK max_retries=0').
findings.suggested_fix: In the transport-injection story (95a5 or the gate-wiring story), add: after constructing the AnthropicProvider with the custom http_client, assert that `provider._client.http_client.transport` is an instance of `AsyncTenacityTransport` (or equivalent check). If the assertion fails, raise a loud ConfigurationError before setting max_retries=0, so the operator sees the misconfiguration instead of experiencing silent retry loss.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly assign a879 to wave 0 or 1 (before any behavior-changing stories) and assign 9a82 to a specific wave slot, updating the merge-order specification in the parent and in child 2eca's integration-ownership section.
findings.finding: The merge-wave slot for children a879 and 9a82 is never explicitly assigned. The parent merge order '0→1→{2,3,4,5}→6' identifies 2eca as wave 6 and names intra-wave edges within {2,3,4,5}, but does not place a879 or 9a82 in any wave. If either ends up in the same wave as 30ff/95a5/fd1d/978d, a879's RED tests run concurrently with stories that flip them GREEN, creating non-deterministic CI results.
findings.location: Parent plan — 'merge order 0->1->{2,3,4,5}->6'; child 2eca-0a1d-45c2-4e38 integration ownership section; children a879-3b64-1be9-4035 and 9a82-3109-29b5-471d (no wave slot stated)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State concrete default values (or at minimum, ranges) for per-request read timeout, per-tool timeout, and step caps in the Architecture Constraints section, so the implementing executor has actionable numbers rather than 'generous'.
findings.finding: Timeout values (per-request read timeout, per-tool timeout, step caps) are referenced throughout the plan and ACs but no concrete values, ranges, or even ballpark defaults are stated anywhere in the epic.
findings.location: Architecture constraints ('generous per-request read timeout + per-tool timeout + step caps'), Success Criteria (4th bullet), Acceptance Criteria (5th bullet)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'Defaults: per-request read timeout 120 s, per-tool timeout 60 s, step cap 50; all three are config-overridable. CI tests may lower these values via model_override to avoid slow tests.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a reproducible verification command (parallel to the `AsyncTenacityTransport` check) confirming the exact callable name and signature for Retry-After support in `pydantic_ai.retries` at 1.107.0 (e.g., `python -c "from pydantic_ai.retries import wait_retry_after; import inspect; print(inspect.signature(wait_retry_after))"`), and record the confirmed name in the Architecture constraints section.
findings.finding: The `wait_retry_after` symbol inside `pydantic_ai.retries` is asserted as a usable export alongside `AsyncTenacityTransport`, but unlike `AsyncTenacityTransport` no verification command is provided for it. Given that prior review passes misidentified the entire `[retries]` extra as nonexistent, a specific symbol-level verification is warranted for the Retry-After helper specifically.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point' paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Architecture constraints block: `python -c "import pydantic_ai.retries as r; print([x for x in dir(r) if 'retry' in x.lower() or 'after' in x.lower()])"` to enumerate the actual Retry-After-related exports, then pin the confirmed name.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify what 'confirmed additive-safe' means as an artifact: e.g., 'a committed analysis note in docs/ listing each consumer, its exit-code handling logic (source-cited), and a one-line verdict that exit 11 does not change its behavior; OR a test asserting the consumer script treats any-non-zero as failure.'
findings.finding: 'Confirmed additive-safe' for exit-code consumers (CI, Gerrit reviewer-bot) is vague: no artifact, analysis document, or test is specified that constitutes 'confirmation', making this AC unevaluable in-session.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, 9th bullet: 'exit-code consumers (CI, Gerrit reviewer-bot) enumerated + confirmed additive-safe'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify a879's liveness scenario intent: explicitly state each liveness scenario asserts current broken behavior (e.g. 'hung model request is NOT aborted; runner hangs or raises LLMUnavailableError without classification') so authors write RED tests, not GREEN tests.
findings.finding: child a879's liveness scenario language is self-contradictory: the story states it pins CURRENT behavior (RED), but the liveness section says 'These pin the run_sync-compatible zombie-run defense' — language that implies asserting the defense WORKS (new/green behavior). If an author writes these tests asserting aborted+classified outcomes, the tests will fail CI until fd1d lands, breaking the baseline story's own CI gate.
findings.location: child a879-3b64-1be9-4035 — Scope section: 'Named liveness scenarios: a hung model request (a FunctionModel that sleeps past the per-request read timeout) is aborted+classified; a hung tool... is aborted+classified; a looping model... trips the step cap. These pin the run_sync-compatible zombie-run defense.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'These pin the run_sync-compatible zombie-run defense' with 'These pin the ABSENCE of the zombie-run defense in current code (i.e. a hung request currently hangs or raises generically; fd1d flips these GREEN when it adds the timeout mechanism).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC line that explicitly verifies the diagnostic sanitization implementation: e.g., 'Diagnostic output (logs, CLI stderr, session log, verdict) is scrubbed of secrets/credentials/PII before emission, asserted by a test that injects a mock diagnostic containing a credential-shaped string and confirms it does not appear in any sink.'
findings.finding: Diagnostic sanitization is described as an implementation deliverable ('scrubbed of secrets/credentials/PII before any sink') but no AC verifies the implementation is built and tested — only the ADR recording it is covered.
findings.location: Architecture Constraints ('Diagnostic sanitization' paragraph) and AC bullet 10 ('ADRs are recorded for… the diagnostic-sanitization contract')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC: '- [ ] The verbatim diagnostic is scrubbed of secrets/credentials/PII before emission to any sink (logs, CLI stderr, session log, verdict); a unit test asserts that a mock diagnostic containing a bearer-token-shaped string is redacted in all output paths.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Cite at least one reference taxonomy (e.g., Anthropic API error types, OpenAI error codes, or a recognized LLM-ops framework's error classification) that the 8-class disposition set was compared against, and document which classes are adopted from prior art vs. rebar-specific additions — even if one sentence per novel class in the child story or ADR.
findings.finding: The 8-class resolution-disposition taxonomy (WAIT_AND_RETRY, RETRY_NOW, CHANGE_SETTINGS, CHANGE_INPUT, CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL, INCREASE_PROVIDER_LIMITS, FIX_AGENT_DESIGN, NEEDS_INVESTIGATION) is presented as 'grounded in how the OSS community solves these problems,' but no specific reference implementation or prior-art system with a comparable classification taxonomy is cited. Classes like CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL and INCREASE_PROVIDER_LIMITS appear to be novel inventions for this plan; the plan doesn't demonstrate they are drawn from an established pattern.
findings.location: Context — 'Classify what survives retries' bullet; Acceptance Criteria — 'closed resolution-disposition enum' AC

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly declare whether `resolution_class` is an optional/nullable addition to the verdict schema (additive, no consumer migration needed) or a required field (breaking, requiring an expand-contract sequence), and enumerate any verdict-JSON consumers that must handle the new field.
findings.finding: The verdict schema gains a `resolution_class` field (referenced as 'the resolution_class carried on the verdict coverage'), but the plan does not state whether this field is optional/nullable (additive, backward-compatible) or required (potentially breaking) for existing verdict consumers — e.g., downstream parsers, the session log reader, or CI tools that deserialize verdict JSON.
findings.location: Architecture constraints — 'Exit-code signal' bullet; Success Criteria; Acceptance Criteria bullet 4.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a config key (e.g. `llm.retry.enabled`, defaulting `true`) or environment variable that disables the transport-layer retry entirely, so operators can validate or emergency-revert without a code release.
findings.finding: The new transport-layer retry behavior (AsyncTenacityTransport, 529 handling, Retry-After cap) has no feature flag or opt-out mechanism; rollback for this behavioral change requires a code revert rather than a config toggle, leaving CI consumers with no staged path to validate the change before it affects all runs.
findings.location: Architecture constraints — 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point'; AC: 'Retry is owned at exactly one layer (the httpx transport), SDK max_retries=0'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `llm.retry.enabled: bool = True` to the config schema (owned by the gate-wiring story as single source of truth for the config namespace). When `False`, inject the plain `httpx.AsyncClient` instead of `AsyncTenacityTransport`. Assert in tests that `--force` and `retry.enabled=False` both bypass the retry path. Document the key alongside the taxonomy/retry/timeout docs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic,retries]` resolves cleanly (combined-extra form), that the tenacity transitive version is compatible with the dep tree, and explicitly address whether `tests/unit/workflow/test_executor_tripwire.py`'s tenacity ban-list entry needs updating when tenacity becomes a real transitive dep.
findings.finding: The plan adds `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` as a hard prerequisite to the `[agents]` extra (currently `pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic]>=1,<2`), but does not verify that the `[retries]` extra's tenacity version pin is compatible with the rest of the dependency tree, nor whether `pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic,retries]` (the combined form) is the correct spelling or whether the two extras interact cleanly.
findings.location: Architecture constraints — 'pydantic-ai-slim[retries] is a hard prerequisite'; Acceptance Criteria — 'pydantic-ai-slim[retries] is added as a dependency'
findings.suggested_fix: Run `pip install 'pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic,retries]' --dry-run` to confirm the combined form and tenacity version; add a note to the spike story's AC to update the tripwire ban-list if needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the Retry-After cap value (e.g., '60 s') in the Architecture Constraints or Context section so the executor has a concrete default to implement against.
findings.finding: The Retry-After cap is mentioned in both the Context section and Acceptance Criteria as 'honored and capped' but no cap value is stated, leaving the executor to choose a number with no guidance.
findings.location: Context section ('Retry-After honored and capped'), Acceptance Criteria (2nd bullet: 'Retry-After is honored and capped')
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Retry-After is honored up to a cap of 60 s (configurable); requests with Retry-After > 60 s are classified WAIT_AND_RETRY and not retried inline.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether verdict artifacts (session-log entries, on-disk verdict files) are ephemeral-per-run or re-read across deployments; if re-read, specify that `resolution_class` and any other new verdict fields are added as nullable/optional (expand-contract), document the post-rollback schema state, and confirm existing records without the field remain valid.
findings.finding: The plan adds a `resolution_class` field to the verdict `coverage` schema (and potentially other verdict-schema fields for silent-success detection), but does not address whether previously-persisted verdict artifacts (session logs, on-disk verdict files) are forward/backward compatible or require a migration step. If any consumer reads historical verdict records, the absence of the new field could cause parse failures or silent misclassification.
findings.location: Architecture constraints — 'the verdict schemas…are touched by multiple children'; AC — 'verdict schema-shape check'; 'resolution_class carried on the verdict coverage'
findings.suggested_fix: Declare `resolution_class` Optional with a default of `None` (or an UNKNOWN sentinel) in the Pydantic verdict model so old records deserialize cleanly. Document that rollback leaves the field in the schema as Optional (no destructive DDL), so no data loss occurs on partial deployment.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'de-risked by a spike' with a specific observable artifact: name the committed file or test (e.g., 'story 95a5 produces a committed integration test in tests/integration/test_transport_retry_spike.py that injects a 429→200 sequence and asserts the response is the 200 without duplicate tool calls').
findings.finding: The Acceptance Criterion for the transport-injection spike is effort-framed ('de-risked by a spike') rather than specifying the observable artifact that proves de-risking, making it impossible to objectively verify completion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, 8th bullet: 'The transport-injection integration point is de-risked by a spike before dependent stories build on it'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete rollback/back-out sequence for the exit-11 change: either an expand-contract window (emit both exit-2 and exit-11 for N releases, then drop exit-2 for this case) or explicit revert steps (restore exit-2 for retryable-INDETERMINATE, un-pin exit-11 from the test, notify enumerated consumers), not just a one-liner.
findings.finding: The exit-11 rollback is stated as a one-liner parenthetical ('rollback = map the retryable subset back to exit 2') with no concrete back-out steps or expand-contract sequencing. T4 requires the reversibility *mechanism*, not just acknowledgement of the option.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Exit code 11 amends a FROZEN contract' paragraph; Acceptance Criteria bullet 9
findings.suggested_fix: In the gate-wiring story (the named integrator), add a 'Rollback procedure' subsection: (1) revert _llm_commands.py to map WAIT_AND_RETRY/RETRY_NOW back to exit 2; (2) revert docs/exit-codes.md entry; (3) revert the test_exit_codes.py pin for 11; (4) notify CI and Gerrit reviewer-bot owners. Optionally, consider a short expand-contract window where exit-11 is emitted and logged but exit-2 is also returned for one release cycle.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit budget-exhaustion entry to the classifier spec (mapping Anthropic's credit-limit error body / HTTP 402 to `INCREASE_PROVIDER_LIMITS` or a dedicated disposition), include a corresponding AC line, and add a test scenario that injects a budget-exceeded response via `model_override` and asserts the expected disposition and actionable diagnostic.
findings.finding: 'Budget' failures are named in the plan title and epic subtitle as a first-class failure mode to handle, but the plan body contains no explicit treatment of budget exhaustion: no AC line names it, no classification entry maps a budget-exceeded HTTP status (402 or provider-specific error body) to a disposition, and no test scenario requires it to be exercised.
findings.location: Title; Success Criteria; Acceptance Criteria; Architecture constraints — disposition enum list

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify minimum sanitization coverage in the AC (or a dedicated section): enumerate concrete pattern classes that MUST be scrubbed (Authorization/x-api-key headers, bearer tokens, prompt fragments in error bodies), require fail-closed behaviour (if sanitizer raises, block the sink, not bypass), and add at least one AC line requiring a test that injects a synthetic diagnostic containing a fake secret and asserts it does not appear in any sink.
findings.finding: The diagnostic-sanitization contract is acknowledged but its minimum requirements are left entirely to an ADR with no specified coverage criteria, no fail-closed behaviour requirement, and no test AC — leaving the boundary-crossing path (Anthropic API error responses → logs/stderr/verdicts) potentially able to leak secrets or PII if the sanitizer is incomplete or absent.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Diagnostic sanitization' bullet; AC item 'ADRs are recorded for…diagnostic-sanitization contract'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC: 'The diagnostic sanitizer MUST scrub at minimum: (a) values matching known secret-header names (x-api-key, Authorization, Bearer …), (b) any string matching the configured API key value; it MUST fail closed (sanitization failure → emit a redacted placeholder, not the raw string); a unit test MUST assert that a synthetic diagnostic embedding a fake API-key value produces no output containing that value at any sink (log, stderr, verdict).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the classifier's assignment rules: provide a table mapping exception types/HTTP codes (429, 529, 5xx, auth, timeout, budget, StructuredOutputError, UnretryableOutputError, etc.) to each enum value, and specify which values are retryable (→ exit 11) vs not (→ exit 2 INDETERMINATE).
findings.finding: The resolution-disposition enum has 8 named values (WAIT_AND_RETRY, RETRY_NOW, CHANGE_SETTINGS, CHANGE_INPUT, CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL, INCREASE_PROVIDER_LIMITS, FIX_AGENT_DESIGN, NEEDS_INVESTIGATION) that are central to routing (exit 11 vs exit 2, actionable messaging), but the plan never defines the classifier's assignment rules — which HTTP status codes, exception types, or error signatures map to each value.
findings.location: Plan body § 'Classify what survives retries'; AC line '[ ] Every LLM failure is classified into the closed resolution-disposition enum'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Classifier mapping' section (or table in the child story) listing each resolution class, the exception/status code pattern that triggers it, and whether it is retry-eligible. Example: 429 → WAIT_AND_RETRY (retry), 529 → WAIT_AND_RETRY (retry), 5xx → RETRY_NOW (retry), auth/401 → CHANGE_SETTINGS (no retry), budget-exceeded → CHANGE_SETTINGS (no retry), UnretryableOutputError → CHANGE_INPUT (no retry), unknown → NEEDS_INVESTIGATION (no retry by default).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `coverage` subfields added by this epic (at minimum `resolution_class` and `diagnostic`) for all three verdict schemas, and add `coverage` to completion_verdict.schema.json if it is missing — the uniform three-gate posture requires it.
findings.finding: The `resolution_class` field is said to live on the verdict `coverage` object and drive exit-code bifurcation (exit 11 vs 2), but all three verdict schemas define `coverage` as `{"type": "object", "additionalProperties": true}` with no subfield specification — the plan never documents what fields are added to `coverage`, which schema file(s) are amended, or what the structural guard on signing checks.
findings.location: Architecture Constraints § 'Exit-code signal'; plan_review_verdict.schema.json:46-50; code_review_verdict.schema.json:30-34; completion_verdict.schema.json (no coverage field)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'schema-guided json-repair' and 'faulty-output-in-reask' as concrete behavioral specs: what each tweak changes relative to the existing structured.py ladder (layers 1-4), the trigger condition, the expected output, and which child story owns each.
findings.finding: The two named 'output self-heal tweaks' — 'schema-guided json-repair' and 'faulty-output-in-reask' — are listed in the silent-success layer without any definition of what change they represent to the existing structured.py ladder, when they are triggered, or what protocol governs them.
findings.location: Plan body § 'Silent-success layer': 'plus two additive output self-heal tweaks (schema-guided json-repair; faulty-output-in-reask) on top of rebar's already-strong structured.py ladder'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the detection protocol for refusal-as-content: what heuristic(s) (schema field absence, keyword probe, verdict-shape check, etc.) are used to detect a model refusal that arrives with stop_reason=end_turn/stop, and what error class/routing it produces.
findings.finding: The silent-success layer lists 'structured refusal-as-content detection' as a new capability, but the plan does not specify the detection heuristic for the case where a model returns refusal prose as message body with a normal stop_reason (end_turn/stop) — the existing check_stop_reason() only covers the case where the model properly signals stop_reason=refusal.
findings.location: Plan body § 'Silent-success layer': 'structured refusal-as-content detection'; src/rebar/llm/structured.py:213-245

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
